text: Genesis chapter number three, if you will, Genesis chapter number three, we'll be looking at verses one through seven this evening. Genesis chapter number three, verses one through seven, and I'll give you a moment to turn there, the title for the message this evening, Sin Entered the World. Verse number one of Genesis three, the Bible says, now the serpent was more subtle than any beast of the field which the Lord God had made. And he said unto the woman, yea, hath God said, ye shall not eat of every tree of the garden. The woman said unto the serpent, we may eat of the fruit of the trees of the garden. But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die. And the serpent said unto the woman, ye shall not surely die. For God doth know that in the day ye eat thereof, then your eyes shall be opened, and ye shall be as gods, knowing good and evil. When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise, she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her, and he did eat. And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked, and they sewed fig leaves together, and made themselves aprons. As you understand, as we've studied through the book of Genesis up to this point, we spent a couple of weeks looking at the second chapter of Genesis, and we understand that Adam and Eve, as they spent time in the garden, it is not a specified period of time that God gives us as to how long they were in the Garden of Eden. As we study chapter number two, we do know that they were placed there by God. The garden was specifically created by God and set there for a place that was prepared for Adam and Eve according to verse number eight of chapter two. We find that God planted that garden in that place for that purpose. As we think about the period of time in which Adam and Eve lived in the garden, and it's an unspecified period of time, we'd understand this evening as we'd read the text of Scripture and getting into the third chapter of the book of Genesis, that it would lead us to believe that the period of time was not very long that they spent in the garden. The period of time was distinguished, as we looked at last week, as a time of innocency, a time in which God created everything perfect, and it was that period of innocency. It's understood, as we look at the third chapter of the book of Genesis, that evidently for however long they had spent in the garden, they did experience fellowship with God. You understand that in chapter number three, in verse number eight, it says, when they heard the voice of the Lord God walking in the garden in the cool of the day, and so evidently they had experienced fellowship with God during that period of time that they were there in the garden. You'll recall from our study over the last couple of Sunday evenings that Genesis chapter number two tells us of many trees that were planted in the garden, but he mentions two specifically by name in verse number nine of Genesis two. It was the tree of life, and it was the tree of knowledge of good and evil. These are the two trees that are mentioned specifically by name. We know that the Bible tells us in verse number nine that God, out of the ground, made the Lord God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight and good for food, and so there are many more trees than the two that are specified by name, but we do know that the tree of life and the tree of knowledge of good and evil were there in the garden. We also know from our study of chapter number two that it's in Genesis chapter number two where God records for us and reveals to us the very first command from God to man. That command is found for us as we would look at the second chapter in verses sixteen and seventeen where the Bible says, and the Lord God commanded the man, saying, of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat, but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil thou shalt not eat of it. From the day that thou eatest thereof, notice, thou shalt surely die. As we understand Genesis chapter number two and the record that is given there in that first command, we said that it was a simple command and there were also known consequences that God gave with regard to that last part of verse number seventeen, thou shalt surely die. As we enter into the third chapter, we see as we've titled the lesson this evening, sin entered the world, reminded of what the New Testament speaks about in Romans five and verse number twelve concerning this sin entering into the world, and Paul puts it this way in Romans five and verse number twelve with regard to the historicity and the accuracy of that which we find in the Genesis record, he says, wherefore as by one man sin entered into the world. And then he says this, and death by sin, and so death passed upon all men for that all have sinned. And we find that Paul, in writing to those in Rome, he references back to this event in the garden that we're studying this evening in chapter number three, when sin entered the world. Now as we look at the passage of scripture in Genesis chapter number three, if you're taking notes this evening, I want you to notice the conversation recorded. In verses one through five, we find that God records for us a conversation, the conversation is between Eve and the serpent, the serpent of course is Satan. We find that the Bible records for us this conversation and we understand as we've mentioned already by way of introduction that this was a perfect environment. We understand that God had created everything in perfection, in fact, in Genesis chapter number one, remember in verse number thirty-one, it says, and God saw everything that he had made and behold it was very good. And we find that the creation week had ended, God gave us the record of the place in which man would dwell, and in that place of perfection, for an unknown period of time they dwelled there. I would believe that it was a short period of time, and now the serpent who is more subtle than any beast of the field, which the Lord God had made, Satan comes to tempt Eve. We find that here as we would understand that Satan is there and in the garden and we'd understand from the passage of scripture that this is the first temptation of mankind. Now here's a question that's asked often and a question that we would seek to answer this evening, though the answer may not please you because the bottom line is we don't know for sure, but the question often asked is regarding Satan, when was he created and when did he fall? And many people ask that question, they try to pinpoint it as to a particular time. There's some Bible scholars that have tried to pinpoint it to an exact day. One in particular said it was the tenth day after creation. I'm not sure how he arrived to his conclusion, but that's what he arrived to. What we'd find this evening as we seek to answer that question, there are some things that the Bible tells us in regard to Satan and the fact that he was created and all of that sort of thing. We know that he was created for over in the book of Ezekiel in chapter number 28, there in that text of scripture, first of all let me mention this, as you're turning to Ezekiel 28, remember over in Exodus in chapter number 20 the Bible says that God made all things in six days and he created all things in six days and so we would understand according to the text of scripture as we look at Ezekiel 28 that Satan was created, he was not created however as Satan, right? We understand that he was Lucifer and that anointed cherub and we find here in Ezekiel chapter number 28 beginning in verse number 13, the Bible says, thou hast been in Eden, the garden of God. Every precious stone was thy covering, the sardis, the topaz, and the diamond, the beryl, the onyx, the jasper, the sapphire, the emerald, and the carbuncle, and gold. The workmanship of thy tabards and of thy pipes were prepared in thee, notice this, in the day that thou wast created and so we understand biblically that Lucifer was created. You find that it says in verse 14, thou art the anointed cherub that covereth and I have set thee so, thou wast upon the holy mountain of God, thou hast walked up and down in the midst of the stones of fire, thou wast perfect in thy ways, notice this, from the day that thou wast created till iniquity was found in thee. By the multitude of thy merchandise they have filled the midst of thee with violence and thou hast sinned therefore I will cast thee as profane out of the mountain of God and I will destroy thee O covering cherub from the midst of the stones of fire. As we understand the passage of scripture here in the book of Ezekiel we would conclude that yes indeed Lucifer was created. Now we also know this that the angels joined in praise when God created the earth. Over in Job chapter number 38 the Bible would give us some indication in that passage of scripture. You remember as you're turning there that God begins to speak in Job 38. Remember Job is seeking an answer from God and there's a period of silence and the time in which his friends are telling him all of these things and of course he concludes that they're miserable comforters and what have you. But we get to Job 38 and we find that the Bible says, then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind and said, but I want to draw your attention specifically to verses six and seven where the Bible says this, whereupon are the foundations thereof fastened or who laid the cornerstone thereof. book of Genesis that it was on day number three when God set the foundation the land and the sea and all of those things and so we would conclude this evening that certainly Lucifer and all of the verse number six and we see the course of sin we find that it begins with the eye there when she saw it the Bible says when the woman saw that the tree was good for food and we can think about and we'll look at a passage of Scripture in 1st John here in just a moment but we can think about the the lust of the flesh. And we see that here it continues and says, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, the lust of the eyes. And then it concludes by saying that it was a fruit that was a tree to be desired to make one wise, the pride of life. We find here that this course of sin that is mentioned here in Genesis 3 and verse 6, that she saw it, she desired it. Eventually, as we look in the text of Scripture, the Bible says that she took of the fruit thereof and did eat. In 1 John 2, the Bible would give us this truth with regard to that which we've just mentioned by parallel of what takes place here in Genesis 3 and verse 6. And we find that in 1 John 2 and verses 15-17, the Bible says, love not the world, neither the things that are in the world. Any man love the world, the love of the Father is not in him. For all that is in the world, the lust of the flesh, we noticed that right when she saw it, the lust of the flesh and the lust of the eyes, it was pleasant to the eyes is what we are told in Genesis 3 and verse 6, and the pride of life. It was desired to make one wise. The Bible says it's not of the Father, but is of the world. And then it tells us this, and the world passeth away in the lust thereof, but he that doeth the will of God abideth forever. As we notice the course of sin here in Genesis 3 and verse 6, we find that the action that has taken place there at the end of the verse of Scripture where she took and ate, that that is the result of standing there, having that conversation, seeing it, and desiring it, and eventually taking it. It reminds me of the course of sin even in the life of Achan as you'd find in Joshua 7. In fact, in Joshua 7, God calls through Joshua, Achan before him, and there's a conversation that takes place, and Joshua says give glory to God, and there's this conversation, and Achan tells him, Joshua in verse 21 of Joshua 7, when I saw among the spoils, he saw it, a goodly Babylonish garment and 200 shekels of silver and a wedge of gold of 50 shekels weight. Then I coveted them, that desire that he had, and it says took them, and behold, they are hid in the earth in the midst of my tent and the silver under it. In Genesis 3, when sin entered into the world, we find this course of sin that is mentioned to us in verse number 6, and we find that this is a course that is often repeated where we see something. Oh, how important it is that we're careful with that which we look at. That we understand the lust of the flesh. That we understand the lust of the eyes. That we understand the pride of life, and that we're careful to guard against those things. That we would say, get thee behind me, Satan. That we would understand that it's important that we know what thus saith the Lord. It's important for us to understand that God is not trying to keep anything from us. Too many people have the wrong idea this evening in our society as I have conversation and interact with individuals, and they'll say, well, God wants me to be happy. Can I tell you that's not God's primary concern for you? God's primary concern for you is not that you're happy. Can I tell you what leads to joy is obedience, right? What leads to joy is doing the will of God, is doing what God has prescribed in His Word. And this idea that, well, God wants me to be happy, and if it makes me happy, then obviously God is for it. No, my friend, you need to go to what the Word of God says. Amen? You need to go and find what did God say in His Word concerning that. And God said to Adam and Eve, notice the grace of God. He says that of all the trees that were there, in verse number 16, He says of chapter number two, of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat. But there was one restriction. Why is it that that one thing that's restricted is something that seems to cause us to stumble so often? And how we'd understand the consequence of sin, and we'd see even here in Genesis 3, when sin entered the world, that we'd understand the grave consequence of this action that is here. It's been recorded for us so that we'd understand. In verse number 6, at the end of the verse of Scripture, it says, She gave also unto her husband with her, and he did eat. As we'd understand in the New Testament again, we mentioned already from the book of Romans in chapter number 5, about Adam there and sin entering into the world. But Paul also says this over in 1 Timothy, chapter number 2, in verse number 14. It's an interesting verse of Scripture. And he's dealing with some things with the church and roles in the church and so on and so forth. But he says here in 1 Timothy 2, in verse number 14, and Adam was not deceived, but the woman being deceived was in the transgression. Eve wasn't, excuse me, Eve was deceived, but Adam was not deceived. In 1 Corinthians 15, in verse number 33, excuse me, verse number 22, God mentions this, the apostle Paul, of course, speaking to the believers in Corinth, but we understand that he's speaking about the resurrection and speaking about the joy of understanding the resurrection because Christ is risen from the dead. But he says this in verse 22 of 1 Corinthians 15, for as in Adam, all die. Why is it in Adam? Because he took of it, and of course we understand the seed and what have you that has passed. And we look here at what takes place. He says, even so in Christ shall all be made alive. And we see in 1 Corinthians 15, as well as Romans chapter number five, the comparison between the first Adam and the second Adam, the Lord Jesus Christ. And we find that as we look at that comparison, that throughout the New Testament, the event that we're looking at this evening in Genesis chapter number three is looked to as being factual, historical event that took place. This is not just some fable. This is not just some story that somebody came up with. This is the actual record of when sin entered the world. And it's interesting because if you understand the first seven verses of Genesis chapter number three, it really unlocks for us the rest of the Bible. Because from Genesis chapter number three all the way to Revelation chapter number 22, what you begin to find is what the consequences were of this event that took place in those first seven verses and how it adversely affected mankind and what God did to reconcile man to himself. And from here all the way through the end, you begin to look and you begin to understand that, listen, if Genesis three, one through seven wasn't here, a lot of it just wouldn't make sense. Right? Why? Why did Jesus come? Why did this happen? Why did that? Why the blood? We have no idea. But when we read these seven verses and we understand the historical accuracy of these verses and we understand the way in which the New Testament writers often refer back to this event that we're studying this evening, we find that it was a catastrophic event that caused much turmoil and of course the death, the spiritual separation from mankind to the Creator. I'm thankful that God is the Creator often as we've studied here on Sunday evenings through the book of Genesis, we find and we've made reference to the fact that not only is He Creator, but He is Redeemer. And it's important that we understand the connection here this evening because so often in our society this evening, this thing of God being the Creator, the truth of God being the Creator of all things is being undermined and what happens as you begin, as you remove the Creator, as you remove the account that we're looking at this evening of sin entering the world, all of a sudden you have no need for a Redeemer. But we indeed need a Redeemer. Mankind needs to understand that truth and the society in which we're living. Listen, many people that we come into contact with reminded of what the truth is in 2 Corinthians 4 that the God of this world has blinded the minds of them that believe not. There's much spiritual deception that's taken place in our society today, in our world as a whole and we find that the truth of God's word is so necessary that the light of the gospel would shine in unto them. And so we see the conversation, we see the course of sin in verse number six. Notice the cost of sin according to verse number seven. The Bible says, and the eyes of them both were opened and they knew that they were naked and they sewed fig leaves together and made themselves aprons. We find that immediately here at the end of verse number six, it says that she took the fruit thereof and did eat and gave also unto her husband with her and he did eat and the eyes of them both were opened. What happened? Their eyes were opened. Remember, it's the tree of, not the tree of life, but it's the tree of knowledge of good and evil. It was that tree that God said, you cannot eat of that tree and yet the woman through the temptation, through the deception of the enemy, through Satan, she saw it and she understood that it was pleasant to be desired and she took it and she ate it. Their eyes are opened. The Bible says that they realized that they were naked. If you'll remember at the end of chapter number two, we looked at the sanctity of marriage. week, we understood that in verse number 25, the Bible says that they were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed. But now their shame, because of this nakedness, how do we know that? Because of their actions. Remember in Genesis chapter number 2, there's still that stage of, or that state of innocency. It's a perfect state, there's no sin that's entered in, but now in chapter number 3, when that sin enters into the world, in verse number 6, their eyes are open and they understand this, they realize this, and so what is it that they did? They tried to cover their sin. Isn't that the natural process? We find that they sewed fig leaves together and made themselves aprons. Really, the actions of Adam and Eve, according to verse number 7, it was the works of their hands. And as we go through chapter number 3, and we study over the next couple of Sunday evenings, we'll find it to be understood at the end of the chapter, especially in verses 21 through 24, that Adam and Eve would soon learn that their covering was utterly inadequate. The works of their hands would not suffice. It was not satisfactory. Amen? And we find that there was blood that was shed, for there were, God made coats of skins and clothed them, according to verse number 21, and we understand the indication of the shedding of blood according to that verse of Scripture. We'll study that in more detail as we get to the end of chapter number 3, but I want you to understand this evening, according to verse number 7, that because of this sin that had entered in, they tried with the works of their hands to cover themselves. It is utterly inadequate. Today, there are people that are trying all sorts of things to cover their sin. The Bible says that he that covereth his sin shall not prosper. Amen? There's only one satisfactory, something that will satisfy the demands of a holy God, and that is the blood of Christ. And we understand throughout the Old Testament there was the blood of bulls and goats and so on and so forth that was shed, seeking to show forth the Messiah who would one day come, and it was a shadow, according to Hebrews 10, a shadow of good things to come. And it was through the blood of Christ that was shed on the cross of Calvary that mankind could once and for all understand the truth of redemption. We're not redeemed with corruptible things, according to what Peter said, but we're redeemed with the precious blood of Christ. And here we find in Genesis 3, verses 1 through 7, we find here the high cost of sin. It was fellowship that was broken. You understand that as you look into verse number 8 and you find that they're hiding themselves from the presence of God, that fellowship that they had enjoyed for whatever duration of time they enjoyed it was now broken because of that sin. This evening, it's important for us to understand the cost of sin. Someone said this, that sin will cost you more than you want to pay. I think that's a pretty accurate statement, don't you? And this evening, as we look at Genesis chapter number 3 and we see that conversation in those first five verses and we find the course of sin, what a warning to us, because yes, we see the cost of sin in verse number 7, but what a warning to us as to the work of the enemy. Last week on Sunday morning, we dealt with neither give place to the devil and we find here, as we mentioned several things about our adversary last Sunday morning, but we find here again some of the tactics of the enemy. Yea, hath God said. God wants us to know what he said, but Satan doesn't. Amen? God wants us to be settled on what thus saith the Lord. He wants us to have a working knowledge, if I could use that terminology, of his word. And we find that he will flat-out deny what God has said. Ye shall not surely die. Well, it's not gonna happen to me. I'm not gonna find myself in that place. Be not deceived, right? The Bible is full of those types of statements. Be not deceived. Be not deceived. What so ever man soweth, that shall he also reap. Amen? It is a principle. It is a principle of God's word, sowing and reaping. By the way, it's a positive and a negative principle. When we sow to the spiritual, we'll reap of the spiritual. When we sow to the carnal, we'll reap of the carnal. We understand that in Galatians 6. But here this evening, we find that he then, at times, Satan, the adversary, will try to get us to believe that God is trying to withhold something from us that's good for us. Can I tell you that there was nothing from the tree that was mentioned here, the tree of knowledge of good and evil, there was nothing from that tree that was good for Adam and Eve. Amen? God was, God knowing all things, being omniscient, being loving and merciful and gracious, God was trying in giving that command. He was seeking to help Adam and Eve avoid that thing that would cause a grave consequence. And this evening, God is not trying to keep something good from you that you need. God is trying to protect you from something that will hurt you. Amen? And we need to understand that from this text of scripture, sin entered the world. As we continue through Genesis through over the next couple of weeks, we'll look at some more of the judgments that are handed out and then of course the work of God and in the lives of Adam and Eve at the end of the chapter and that reconciliation that we would call it from those verses. Let's pray together.
